text: The surgeon navigates around the midline, crossing over to the right side, where the right rectus muscle is visible.

The surgeon encounters adhesions and begins to take them down. The rectus sheath on the left side is incised, and the falciform ligament is opened and brought down. A hernia is visible in the epigastric area, identified as a trocar site hernia from a previous inguinal hernia repair.

The surgeon enters the right retrorectus space, revealing a Swiss cheese hernia with a defect in the posterior sheath measuring approximately 15 cm longitudinally by 13 cm wide. A Vicron mesh, sized 15 by 15 cm, is used to cover the defect.

The diaphragm and sternum are visible as the surgeon places a Vicryl mesh over the hernia defect. Initially, a 3-0 V-lock suture is used to secure the mesh, but due to difficulty in pulling it through the mesh, the surgeon switches to a Stratafix suture for easier handling.
The surgeon sutures the edges of the posterior sheath to the Vicryl mesh, adjusting the docking position and inverting the image for better visualization on the ipsilateral side.

The surgeon uses a PDS Stratafix suture to secure the Vicryl mesh over the hernia defect. The mesh is positioned to provide a good overlap, similar to a regular eTEP Rives-Stoppa repair, without performing a transversus abdominis release (TAR).

The laparoscopic view shows the surgeon performing a baseball stitch to secure the mesh. Some areas of the suture are exposed, prompting the placement of figure-eight silk sutures to cover these regions. The surgeon avoids extensive canal stitches due to unsatisfactory appearance.

The surgeon places a 30 cm by 25 cm soft barred mesh over the surgical area, securing it with several figure-eight silk sutures and a baseball stitch technique to ensure the sutures are hidden and not facing the viscera.
